references.title: Predicting academic performance by considering student heterogeneity

extracted.Courses: Undergraduate Pograms in an Aulstralian University
extracted.Features: [Gender, SAS_ADD_CURRENT_SHORT_SAS, AGE_NUM, ATTENDANCE_TYPE_DESC, ATTENDANCE_MODE_SHORT_DESC, AUST_SES, APPL_WAS_FIRST_PREF, IN_MULTI_PROG_ANY_YR, iN_MULT_PROG_THIS_YR, pARENT_1_EDUCATION_CIODE, pARENT_2_EDUCATION_CODE, HIGH_SCHOOL_STATE, ATAR_rank, BOOK_VIEW, CHOICE_VIEW, COURSE_VISIT, FORUM_ADD_DISCUSSION, FORUM_ADD_POST, FORUM_VIEW_DISCUSSION, fORUM_VIEW_FORUM, lESSON_VIEW, OUWIKI_EDIT, OUWIKI_VIEW, QUIZ_ATTEMPT, QUIZ_REVIEW, QUIZ_VIEW, RESOURCE_VIEW]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Discretization happens across different numeric variables and categorized according to the 4 quartiles. 

Since the study includes a general model and multiple sub-models that account for different student subgroups, each student enrollment is included in total in 8 distinct datasets. 

Then, females and males are subsequently separated in 6 additional datasets.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.798 - J48, Precision: 0.682 - NB, Recall: 0.374 - NB, F1-Score: 0.48 - NB, Cohen’s Kappa: 0.43 - NB, AUC: 0.699 - J48}
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a base, course-agnostic model athat also does not consider student sub-groups and, later, compares the performance of this base model against different models created specifically for different sub-groups. 

Sub-models seem to outperform ther base model in general, but there is no specific submodel that always outperforms the others.

Also, combining data from different sources - enrollment + logs seems to improve performance of the models.

Results shown only refer to base models.
extracted.Limitations:
Data only refers to a set of domestic students in a very specific department.

Additional categorization of the LMS data would also be benefical.

No early prediction

annotations.text: Work starts with the prediction of student performance from data collected at an Australian University. Enrollment details and LMS data. Authors also make an attempt to consider student heterogeneity. To do that, the authors create models that are specific to different student sub-populations.

Results show that models created on sub-populations outperform a general model trained on all instances. Combining LMS and enrollment also outperforms other options. Moreover, there is no best model for all scenarios, but authors argue tree-based or rule-based models for interpretability purposes.
